title: Accessibility
text: Accessibility Uniface exposes the UI of generated Windows desktop applications to screen readers and automation tools for a number of widgets. By exposing the underlying infrastructure, screen readers and automated testing tools can target supported UI elements, and retrieve information regarding their name, value, and status. For these purposes, a mixture of two different technologies has been used - UI Automation and MSAA (Microsoft Active Accessibility). Accessibility = On | TestMode | Off Default value On File section [upi] Arguments We recommend that you must use the default setting, unless you are testing the application with an automation tool. The application performance is not impacted as the interface is called only by the accessibility clients and testing or automation tools. Value Description On This is a default value. If Accessibility is set as On , the accessibility feature is optimized for screen readers. TestMode If Accessibility is set as TestMode , and when an MSAA client sends a query to the Uniface application, the application returns a unique technical name for the field widget, grid entity, grid cell representation, or other grid objects in the form. For example: NAME.PERSON.ORG.3 Off If Accessibility is set as Off , the accessibility feature is turned off. Testing Uniface with Automation Tools Microsoft provides an API for MSAA that provides methods for exposing information about user interface elements in Windows desktop applications. Although primarily intended for software that makes computers accessible to people with impairments, it is also used by testing tools to provide handles for automated testing. When this setting is present, the name that Uniface returns for supported widgets can be accessed by the get_accName method provided by the IAccessible interface. For more information, consult the Microsoft documentation at https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/dd318466(v=vs.85).aspx . Uniface supports MSAA for testing and Windows screen readers only for the supported widgets. The supported widgets are: EditBox egrid (Grid) ucheckbox (CheckBox) colorbox ucombobox (Combo Box) CommandButton (Only for Representation = Windows or Uniface ) udropdownlist (DropDownList) uhtml (HTML Widget) (Widget property debugport must be set) Label ulistbox (ListBox ) uoutputbox (OutputBox) urtfeditbox (RichEditBox) utabex (TabEx) (Including its child tabs and the tab strip scrollbar) Other widgets may return a technical name, but this name is subject to change. Important: The technical names returned are not appropriate for screen readers and other accessibility tools. Returned Technical Names When set to TestMode , the name returned by MSAA is a technical name that can be used by testing tools, but which are not useful for visually impaired users. Uniface UI Object Returned Technical Name Field-level widget FieldName.EntityName.ModelName.OccurrenceIndex where OccurrenceIndex is the number of the visible occurrence in multi-occurrence list. Grid Widget Objects Grid widget EntityName.ModelName Cell Representation FieldName.EntityName.ModelName.OccurrenceIndex where OccurrenceIndex is the number of the row number in the Grid widget. TopHeaderWindow top_area TopHeaderButton top_heading. Index SideHeaderWindow side_area SideHeaderButton side_heading. Index Viewport field_area CornerArea corner_area CornerButton corner_button Screen Reader support for Uniface Best Practices for Developers While the Uniface framework makes certain that the developer has the required accessibility infrastructure, the developer carries the responsibility of making their applications accessible. In the following section, you will learn how to make your application more accessible to your users. Associated labels The most important attribute of screen readers, beside from the value, is the name of the control. Uniface uses the content of the associated label to expose the name of the control. This is in line with Microsoft's guidelines. For example, an empty Editbox contains no information about what is expected to be filled in. Adding an associated label, such as Age makes the content of the control clear to the end user. Thus, a developer is expected to have an associated label alongside their control, wherever it makes sense. A control can also be self-explanatory and have no need for a label; most commonly seen in drop-downs and list boxes. Adding tooltips Screen readers often need extra help from the developer to accurately explain the role of certain controls. It is possible to add a picture in the grid, but screen readers have no picture-reading capabilities. In those cases, the developer can add a tooltip to further explain the purpose of the control. The tooltip text is exposed through the Help or HelpText attribute. HTML Containers In Web development, there are different Accessibility standards. Developers are advised to comply with the WCAG standards when developing code for HTML widgets. Supported widgets Below is a list of all supported widgets: Label CommandButton (Representation = Native | Uniface | Header) ucheckbox (CheckBox) ucombobox (Combo Box) udropdownlist (DropDownList) ulistbox (ListBox ) EditBox uspinbutton (SpinButton) * colorbox * urtfeditbox (RichEditBox) * HTML Widget * uslider (Slider) RadioButton (umonthcalendar) MonthCalendar utabex (TabEx) * egrid (Grid) * uoutputbox (OutputBox) Note: *: Read the following section for limitations. Limitations Spin Button : The up/down arrows of the spinner are not exposed. The control is still fully functional, as the user can type their preference instead of using the buttons. Colorbox : The Colorbox behaves the same way as the Editbox. The inner text is exposed, but there is not much to be done for the color element; it is a visual element. Rich Editbox : The top panel, containing styling buttons, such as Bold, Italics, Underline, and Text color, is not exposed. Thus, styling text is limited to font and font size, when relying on the screen reader. The text editor behaves the same way as the Editbox. HTML widget : This widget is a Chromium Embedded Framework (CEF) container . The HTML widget is expected to behave in the same way as Chromium-based browsers, such as Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge, and others. While testing, NVDA proved to be a better tool than the Narrator in a mouse mode. Thus, the Narrator setting Read and interact with the screen using the mouse must be used with caution. Tabex (utabex) : The entire infrastructure is exposed. The tabs and scroll buttons appear in case of a tab overflow and the contained form. Uniface tabs do not behave the same way as Windows tabs, meaning that switching the focus between tabs with the arrow keys will not notify Narrator . Thus, the Sync the Narrator cursor and system focus setting is not supported in this control. Grid : This is a complex control and supports the following seven sub-widgets: Editbox Checkbox Dropdown Combobox Button Picture Spinner The entire control is exposed, however, the Narrator's focus does not sync well with the Grid's focus. Thus, the Sync the Narrator cursor and system focus setting is not supported in this control. Related Links AccessibleNameSeparator Defines a string of characters that is used to identify parts of the MSAA accessible name for Uniface interface objects. By default, a dot is used as a name separator. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
